content:
First, one shows that any block of “\(L\)” consecutive 1’s contributes the integer  
\[
R_L \;=\;\underbrace{11\cdots1}_{L\text{‐times}}
\;=\;\frac{10^L-1}9.
\]  
We seek nonnegative integers \((y_1,y_2,y_3)\) so that  
\[
y_1R_1\,+\,y_2R_2\,+\,y_3R_3 \;=\;1000,
\]  
i.e.  
\[
y_1\cdot1\;+\;y_2\cdot11\;+\;y_3\cdot111\;=\;1000.
\]  
For each solution we get a total length  
\[
n \;=\;y_1\cdot1\;+\;y_2\cdot2\;+\;y_3\cdot3.
\]  
Elementary case‐by‐case shows that the solutions are exactly those with  
\[
y_3=0,1,\dots,9,\quad
y_2=0,1,\dots,\Big\lfloor\frac{1000-111y_3}{11}\Big\rfloor,
\]
and \(y_1=1000-11y_2-111y_3\).  One checks that the resulting \(n\) all lie in the arithmetic progressions
\[
n=1000-108y_3\;-\;9\,t,\quad t=0,1,2,\dots
\]
and that these produce exactly the numbers  
\[
n\equiv1\pmod9,\quad 28\le n\le1000,
\]
with the single gap \(n=37\).  Hence there are
\[
\bigl|\{\,n\equiv1\pmod9:28\le n\le1000\}\bigr|
=
\underbrace{(111-3+1)}_{=109}
\;-\;1
\;=\;108
\]
valid values of \(n\).  Therefore \(a=108\) and the sum of its digits is  
\[
1+0+8\;=\;9.
\]
\boxed{9}
